attribution: Original text authored for this fixture, 2026

paragraphs.text: The kettle takes its time, and I let it. Mornings in this house have a shape now: the dog's tail thumping against the cupboard, frost easing off the window in patches, my mother's handwriting on the tea tin I still can't bring myself to replace. Outside, the neighbour scrapes his windscreen with a library card. I count the strokes. Eleven. Same as yesterday. There is a kind of peace that is just repetition you've decided to love, and I am deciding, one kettle at a time.

"The window was locked from the inside," Inspector Halloway said, "which is exactly what bothers me." He turned the key over in his palm as if weighing it. "Locks tell you what people wanted you to think. Dust tells you what actually happened." He crouched, ran a gloved finger along the sill, and held it up clean. "Someone wiped this. Recently. So our careful friend wanted a locked window and no dust — and that, Sergeant, is one want too many."

Mira had been warned that grief makes people strange, but no one had told her it would make her punctual. She arrived everywhere early now — doctor's offices, ferry terminals, her own kitchen at five in the morning — as though some part of her believed her father might still be reached if she simply got there before the rest of the world was awake to prevent it. The house she had inherited disapproved of her. She felt this without embarrassment; it was a house built by people who plastered their walls with horsehair and buried boots in the chimney against bad luck, and it knew an impostor when one slept in its beds. She began repairing it anyway, learning as she went, swearing at instructional videos in the blue light of the unfurnished parlour. The first time she replaced a sash cord and the window rose under her hand — weightless, silent, as if forgiving her — she sat down on the paint-spattered floor and cried for an hour, which she understood, even at the time, had nothing to do with the window. By spring she could glaze, point brickwork, and sister a joist. By summer the house had stopped creaking when she climbed the stairs, or she had stopped hearing it, and she could no longer have said with any confidence which of them had been repaired.

The rope bridge had been condemned by every authority that knew it existed, which was, by Tam's count, two governments, one mining company, and his grandmother. He went across anyway, hand over hand where the planking had given up, the gorge below him full of mist and the noise of water he couldn't see. Halfway over, the wind found him. It came up the canyon like something with an opinion, and the bridge began to swing in long sickening arcs, and Tam did what his grandmother had taught him on calmer ropes: he stopped fighting it, gripped, and rode. "The bridge has been here ninety years," she used to say. "It is not interested in being famous for dropping you." When the swinging eased he went on, counting slats, not looking at the gaps, and hauled himself at last onto rock that did not move, where he lay flat on his back and laughed at the indifferent sky until his ribs hurt. Then he got up, because the whole point of crossing was on this side: a tin box, buried forty years ago under the cairn his grandfather had built, containing — if the family story could be trusted for once in its dramatic life — the claim deed, the photograph, and the letter that began, as his grandmother recited it every winter without ever producing it, "To whichever of you was stubborn enough."

The colony ship wakes me every forty years to check the gardens, and every time, the first thing I do is smell the air. Instruments lie politely; tomato leaves don't. This rotation the hydroponics bay smells right — green, peppery, a little tang of nutrient salts — and I log it before I even read the alerts. There's one. Bay nine's pollinator drones have started doing something the manual calls "emergent route optimization" and I call dancing. I watch them for a long time, little gold knots looping figure-eights no one programmed, and I decide, with the full authority of the only person awake for two light-years in any direction, not to fix it.

"You're making a mountain out of a molehill," Dee said, not looking up from the engine.
"It's a mountain-shaped molehill," said Frank. "The man offered to buy the garage twice in one week. Cash. No survey, no questions."
"So he's keen."
"Keen, nothing. Nobody pays over the odds for a leaky roof and a pit full of rainwater unless they know something we don't." Frank wiped his hands on a rag that made them dirtier. "Mark my words, there's a road going through here, or a rail line, or something. He's not buying the garage. He's buying the ground under it."
Dee finally looked up. "Then we'd better find out what the ground knows," she said, "before we sign anything."

You learn the night shift by its sounds. The bread proofer ticks as it cools, the compressor behind the walk-in clears its throat every twenty minutes, and at 3:40, regular as tide, the man with the greyhound passes the window and lifts two fingers off the leash in greeting. You raise the dough hook back. Tonight the rhythm is off; the greyhound man is late, and you catch yourself standing at the glass with floured hands, watching an empty street, surprised at how much of your life has quietly arranged itself around strangers keeping their schedules. At 3:55 he appears, new coat, same dog, and the night clicks back into true like a wheel finding its groove.

Gran says the orchard is haunted but only in the friendly way, the way a coat is haunted by the shape of your arm. Me and Petra went to check. We sat under the crooked apple tree from after lunch until the bats came out, and nothing happened except a hedgehog, which Petra says doesn't count and I say completely counts. When we told Gran she laughed her big laugh and said the ghost was probably shy of hedgehogs, same as anyone, and gave us bread with plum jam. Petra reckons there's no ghost at all. But I saw Gran look out at that tree after she thought we'd gone up, and say goodnight to it, quiet, like you'd say it to a person, so I reckon Petra can think what she likes.

The pawnshop on Ruckert Street kept honest hours and dishonest everything else. Cole had been told to ask for the owner by name and to refuse, politely, the first cup of coffee. He did both. The old man behind the counter studied him the way jewelers study glass that's pretending to be something better, then reached below the register and set a violin case on the counter. "You're not the man who left it," he said. "No," Cole agreed. "I'm the man who's been looking for it." The old man drummed his fingers once. "Then for both our sakes," he said, sliding the case across, "be also the man who was never here."

After the towers went quiet, the village voted to keep the lighthouse running anyway. Not for ships — there had been no ships in living memory — but because, as old Sefa put it at the meeting, a light you can see from your bedroom window is worth a winter's oil. So the lamp turned, and the beam swept the dark hills instead of the dark sea, and children grew up telling time by it the way their great-grandparents had told it by bells. Travelers came over the pass sometimes, rare as comets, and every one of them said the same thing: that they had walked toward the light for two nights, certain it meant harbor. The villagers never corrected them. It did.
